title: Follow the bouncing arm
wikitext:
[]: Follow the bouncing arm.             Quote Post by Iain_ » Wed Feb 16, 2011 11:18 am Edit: Thank you to BornIn1142 for giving me the answer I was looking for so soon. Hi. How often does someone register on this forum just to say "I know what Gendo says to Ritsuko", and then you never hear from them again? I'm guessing it probably happens enough to be pretty annoying. Sorry, but that's pretty much what I'm here to do. I stumbled on what I believe to be the answer, and then tried to find my theory already mentioned online. It is only because I have not found anyone mentioning what I believe to be the answer, for the reason I believe it to be correct, that I've decided to register here and post. I apologize in advance, as I'm sure someone else must have said this already, but I have not been able to find this point mentioned on this forum. It should also be mentioned that everything in this post is a major spoiler, so I'm going to give a warning here (and in the subtitle), and then not use spoiler tags in the post. So, here we go. For those of you who love the thrill of discovering something on their own, I suggest that you watch this YouTube link over and over again until you see it. The answer, I believe, is written out: (Spoilers! Major Ending Spoilers! Do not watch unless you have seen "End of Evangelion" all the way through.) http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=1mRPRFrvQBg For the rest of you, watch the video, too. You'll be able to go back and compare what I'm about to say to the video. Besides, do you really need an excuse to listen to "Komm, Susser Todd" again? First, notice how Shinji tells Asuka "you're the only person that can help me", to which she responds "Liar". I always believed that this was a hint as to what Gendo said. I, like many of you, believe that the answer to the question is contained within the movie, so I sought out other places where someone responded to a statement with "Liar". Shinji's statement is too long, though. It should be something that expresses need like that, but shorter. Recently I was listening to Komm Susser Todd via the link above. I had just been critiquing someone else' piece of writing where they violated " Conservation of Detail ". Basically, the writer had included a very prominent object in a plot, an object which turned out to be meaningless. I kept waiting for this object to come back into the plot, and it never did. Meanwhile, I missed what was actually going on. Back to Tod. I never understood why the animators / story-boarders / director had Ritsuko type on the keyboard when she appears to Maya Ibuki. It seemed obvious that something would be written there, so I slowed it down and watched it again. What is written on the screen isn't a secret, either; you can find it on Maya's page at several of the wiki sites. In light of Conservation of Detail, though, it seems obvious that this line is an item of importance. It is hidden, but there are plenty of hints for finding it. Why hide something that is only important to a minor character? [I know someone will bring it up, so I should mention here that it is "hidden" in that it is only on screen very briefly, at a time when there is a dismembered limb flying across the screen. Guess where most people's eyes are?] Isn't this what we're all looking for, something hidden, but there for the finding? Seeing as how "I need you" is such a prevalent and recurring theme in EoE, it makes sense that this could be the answer to the question of Gendo and Retsuko. Because this is the only appearance of Retsuko after she dies, and this line can be attributed to her, it seems very possible that this would be where Anno would place the answer. Because "I need you" is basically a shorter way to say "You're the only person that can help me", these two statements would seem to be linked by the response "liar". My answer, then, is that Gendo said either "I truly need you" or "I truly needed you". I am satisfied with this answer, and I'm sure that I'm missing the topic where someone already makes this argument, only much better. I'm not posting here to inflate my ego so that I'll look like god's gift to the forum, or the smartest Eva fan ever, or something like that. I'm just a casual fan who stumbled on this theory, but can't find it mentioned on the internet. I'd like it if someone could link me to a page that explains why I'm wrong, why I could be right, or whatever, but only if it mentions this theory. As I'm satisfied that this answer is correct, I'm looking for comments on this theory, and am not interested in hearing about all the other theories (which I am already pretty well acquainted with).

[]:             Quote Post by BornIn1142 » Wed Feb 16, 2011 11:23 am I've been here for two weeks, but even I know this has been suggested before. An in-depth analysis of the theory even has a page on the wiki. Still, if you didn't know about it, good eye.

[]:             Quote Post by Iain_ » Wed Feb 16, 2011 12:01 pm Thank you for the answer and for the link. Would you believe that some of us really suck that badly when it comes to using Google? I dug through many different threads, both here and elsewhere, looking for a link like that :/ I really like the part about "Is this the answer I was looking for". Well done, Anno.

[]:             Quote Post by Eva Yojimbo » Wed Feb 16, 2011 10:11 pm I still personally prefer the "I have no regrets concerning my work" answer. This is what Gendo tells to Naoko, Ritsuko's mother, in ep. 21, where a young Ritsuko is watching. Naoko responds with "liar!" as well. To me, this always fit better with what Ritsuko's VA said about the line, especially the part about the line "utterly defeating Ritsuko" and Anno being a genius. Because that line, much more than "I needed you", wraps up the entire Ritsuko/Naoko/Gendo triangle, because Gendo is feeding the same line to Ritsuko that he did to her mother, and Ritsuko realizes it, and can only complete the circle by repeating what her mother said. "I needed you" works for the reasons listed in that Wiki page, but I have some problems. First, it's like the second most OBVIOUS answer next to "I love(ed) you", second, Ritsuko's "liar" makes less sense, because Gendo DID need her and she should know it, thirdly, I don't see how the line "utterly defeats Ritsuko" in the slightest. Probably the biggest thing "I needed you" has going for it over "no regrets" is that it fits Gendo's lip movement.

[]:             Quote Post by SahaquielTheTenth » Wed Apr 13, 2011 10:27 am After reading the part on how Ritsu looked down for a moment in reflection before saying "liar" had me convinced that it was "I need you". In my opinion one of the reasons she killed off the spare Rei dummies was because it was beginning all too clear (to her) that it was Rei that Gendo really needed- a physical image of Yui a well as his ticket to be with his wife again. That single moment of realization is what spoke volumes to me. *He threw me out there so he could protect Rei... He needs HER (Rei or Yui).* ..."Liar..."

[]:             Quote Post by toe mash » Wed Apr 13, 2011 10:41 am Eva Yojimbo wrote: I still personally prefer the "I have no regrets concerning my work" answer.

[]:             Quote Post by thewayneiac » Wed Apr 13, 2011 12:14 pm The problem with the "no regrets" theory is that it makes no sense in context for Gendo to say that. Ritsuko neither says nor does anything that could have prompted Gendo to repete that line. It would be repetition simply for repetition's sake.

[]:             Quote Post by Sailor Star Dust » Wed Apr 13, 2011 12:20 pm @ OP: "I need(ed) you" being what Gendo said to Ritsuko has been accepted by some over the "I have no regrets concerning my work" theory, yes. Personally, I think "I need(ed) you" does fit thematically better. More information here, which is what the Wiki page was a copy/paste of: http://forum.evageeks.org/viewtopic.php?p=355299#355299 (Welcome to the forums, btw. :) Feel free to stick around if you'd like.)

[]:             Quote Post by gatotsu911 » Wed Apr 13, 2011 1:34 pm You know, the title of this thread made me think it would be a fun sing-along video involving one or more of Evangelion 's selection of severed arms. I am very, very disappointed that this is not the case.

[]:             Quote Post by Monk Ed » Wed Apr 13, 2011 4:49 pm You know, Eva Yojimbo, although I remember feeling an intense shock of realization when I read your explanation of "no regrets," after reading that wiki page, and Shin-seiki's analysis... I'm convinced. Really, the only reason I never believed it before was because it felt "too" obvious. I mean come on, "I needed you"? How cheesy is that! But now I've seen all the supporting evidence -- and isn't it just like Anno to show us a truth so obvious that we refuse to accept it? (Much like how Shinji refused to accept that Asuka was into him because she was being so obvious :-X.) And, really, when I look back on your explanation with fresh eyes, I'm less convinced -- it's too obscure, and incongruous with everything else that happens in EOE. When an author creates a puzzle that so obviously begs to be solved, he will not bury the clues so deeply that only obsessive fans could solve it -- especially considering that that goes against the entire message of Eva! What sealed it for me, I think, was understanding it from the Japanese audience's perspective. To the Japanese audience, those English words were just as silenced to them as Gendo's line was, understandable only by those with a little extra linguistic training. What the Japanese saw that we didn't, was a parallel that only a native Japanese speaker would see : two silenced lines, one mouthed in Japanese, one written in English, both partially obscured behind a language-related barrier. It is that similarity which would suggest to them that they would share the same answer. I think the questing we have been doing for all these years is because we English speakers didn't see that parallel, between Gendo's silenced line and Ritsuko's typed words. My analysis of why this was so hard to accept is, there is this sort of ... affliction among Eva fandom (including myself) whereby we just can't accept the obvious. We think that the truth must always be far more complex, convoluted, genius, and hidden than we could possibly imagine. The truth can even be presented directly to us and we sometimes still outright refuse it, always believing a greater answer to be lying just over the next hill as we chase those secret rainbows. But in oh so many cases, I think the truth we are seeking is far closer than we, for the sake of our egos or for the sake of our desire for NGE to be always something more , are able to accept.

[]:             Quote Post by Eva Yojimbo » Thu Apr 14, 2011 5:53 am ^ Awwwww, Monk... I am disappoint. Honestly, some may say that I'm "biased" towards the "No regrets" interpretation because I initially put it forward (at least, with the most complete argument), but I don't think that's the reason at all. I like it because it makes much more sense given the relationship between Gendo, Ritsuko, and Naoko, it makes infinitely more sense given what Ritsuko's VA says about the line, and it makes more sense given Ritsuko's response. To nitpick a few points: Monk Ed wrote: when I look back on your explanation with fresh eyes, I'm less convinced -- it's too obscure, and incongruous with everything else that happens in EOE. When an author creates a puzzle that so obviously begs to be solved, he will not bury the clues so deeply that only obsessive fans could solve it -- especially considering that that goes against the entire message of Eva!

[]:             Quote Post by Reichu » Thu Apr 14, 2011 6:00 am I'm not that invested in this argument, but the fact that "hitsuyou datta"/"hitsuyou nanda" (need(ed) you) actually fits in Gendo's mouth (whereas "no regrets" totally wouldn't) seems to tilt the scale heavily against Jimbo here.

[]:             Quote Post by symbv » Thu Apr 14, 2011 6:02 am I second Reichu. Besides, "No regrets" would be too plain and flat to shock Ritsuko so that she had to pause to reply "Liar!".

[]:             Quote Post by Eva Yojimbo » Thu Apr 14, 2011 6:16 am Reichu wrote: I'm not that invested in this argument, but the fact that "hitsuyou datta"/"hitsuyou nanda" (need(ed) you) actually fits in Gendo's mouth (whereas "no regrets" totally wouldn't) seems to tilt the scale heavily against Jimbo here.

[]:             Quote Post by symbv » Thu Apr 14, 2011 6:28 am Eva Yojimbo wrote: Errr, Ritsuko hearing the same line fed to her as was fed to her mother by the same man (that she loved back when her mother was involved with him) and realizing that she's basically BECOME her mother is "too plain and flat"? And why would "I need(ed) you" be so shocking?

[]:             Quote Post by Monk Ed » Thu Apr 14, 2011 6:40 am Eva Yojimbo wrote: But Ritsuko neither says nor does anything that would've prompted Gendo to say "I needed you" either, so that argument is rather silly.

[]:             Quote Post by Eva Yojimbo » Fri Apr 15, 2011 6:06 am I must say, Monk Ed, your explanation/interpretation is as close as anyone's ever come to convincing me of this. At least, I think you lay it out on a much deeper and more significant level than anyone else has. I was actually thinking about this a lot yesterday after I got offline (I read your post before) and, the more I thought about it, the more I realize that both interpretations have nearly identical elements going for it. 1. Foreshadowing/Echoes -Need is foreshadowed in the last Gendo/Ritsuko encounter, and even echoed on Maya's computer, Regrets in the ep. 21 scene. Need has the advantage of being more recent, Regrets has the advantage of being more similar (in respect to the "liar!" line). Advantage: Push 2. Characters' relationships -I think you laid out how Need fits the Gendo/Ritsuko dynamic on a much deeper level than I ever realized, and I have no more to add to what you said. However, Regrets much more elegantly ties up the Naoko/Gendo/Ritsuko triangle. Advantage: Push, with slight advantage to Regrets, I think. 3. Series' themes -Need is more of a common theme in general, but Regrets has more of a metafictional connotation, especially if we consider Anno is speaking through Gendo. Advantage: Push, with slight advantage to Need, I think. 4. More "Gendo" -I think Regrets wins decidedly here, as it has more of a Han Solo "I know" vibe to it, which fits with Gendo's reluctance to show any weakness. Advantage: Regrets 5. Lip movement -Obviously, one of the clear major advantages for Need Advantage: Need If I were to add all of these up, it would be pretty close to 50/50 considering exactly how we scored them. Although, for the first time, I think I'm slightly tilted more towards Need, but this still isn't to say that I'm certain or that I'm definitively in favor of it. I still favor Regrets when it comes to the character dynamics and wrapping up the triangle, and I like that its foreshadowing seems more precise and less general than Need.

[]:             Quote Post by symbv » Fri Apr 15, 2011 6:17 am Actually I think Need is even more elegant in wrapping up the triangle, particularly if Ritsuko was expecting to be treated the same way as Naoko by Gendou but in fact got something entirely different and something which she never dreamt would come from Gendou (but she desired). This would gave be the real shocker for Ritsuko and by hearing that she knew she was not going the way of her mother. The cursed parallel is thus broken and she had no regret to accept her death. From a narrative point of view my humble opinion is that it is an elegant and satisfactory resolution for the triangle.

[]:             Quote Post by Eva Yojimbo » Fri Apr 15, 2011 6:23 am To me, that perspective seems really counter-intuitive. Why would Ritsuko, who hated her mother, go after Gendo if she expected to be treated with as much dismissal and disdain? Ritsuko's entire thing was that she DIDN'T want to be her mother, and, like most people have a tendency to do, she thought she was special and that her love for Gendo would overcome his coldness and obsession for Yui. For Ritsuko, there could be nothing more devastating than realizing she was no better than her mother.

[]:             Quote Post by Monk Ed » Fri Apr 15, 2011 6:35 am Eva Yojimbo wrote: I must say, Monk Ed, your explanation/interpretation is as close as anyone's ever come to convincing me of this. At least, I think you lay it out on a much deeper and more significant level than anyone else has.

Article: Follow the bouncing arm
 15, 2011 6:06 am I must say, Monk Ed, your explanation/interpretation is as close as anyone's ever come to convincing me of this. At least, I think you lay it out on a much deeper and more significant level than anyone else has. I was actually thinking about this a lot yesterday after I got offline (I read your post before) and, the more I thought about it, the more I realize that both interpretations have nearly identical elements going for it. 1. Foreshadowing/Echoes -Need is foreshadowed in the last Gendo/Ritsuko encounter, and even echoed on Maya's computer, Regrets in the ep. 21 scene. Need has the advantage of being more recent, Regrets has the advantage of being more similar (in respect to the "liar!" line). Advantage: Push 2. Characters' relationships -I think you laid out how Need fits the Gendo/Ritsuko dynamic on a much deeper level than I ever realized, and I have no more to add to what you said. However, Regrets much more elegantly ties up the Naoko/Gendo/Ritsuko triangle. Advantage: Push, with slight advantage to Regrets, I think. 3. Series' themes -Need is more of a common theme in general, but Regrets has more of a metafictional connotation, especially if we consider Anno is speaking through Gendo. Advantage: Push, with slight advantage to Need, I think. 4. More "Gendo" -I think Regrets wins decidedly here, as it has more of a Han Solo "I know" vibe to it, which fits with Gendo's reluctance to show any weakness. Advantage: Regrets 5. Lip movement -Obviously, one of the clear major advantages for Need Advantage: Need If I were to add all of these up, it would be pretty close to 50/50 considering exactly how we scored them. Although, for the first time, I think I'm slightly tilted more towards Need, but this still isn't to say that I'm certain or that I'm definitively in favor of it. I still favor Regrets when it comes to the character dynamics and wrapping up the triangle, and I like that its foreshadowing seems more precise and less general than Need.

[]:             Quote Post by symbv » Fri Apr 15, 2011 6:17 am Actually I think Need is even more elegant in wrapping up the triangle, particularly if Ritsuko was expecting to be treated the same way as Naoko by Gendou but in fact got something entirely different and something which she never dreamt would come from Gendou (but she desired). This would gave be the real shocker for Ritsuko and by hearing that she knew she was not going the way of her mother. The cursed parallel is thus broken and she had no regret to accept her death. From a narrative point of view my humble opinion is that it is an elegant and satisfactory resolution for the triangle.

[]:             Quote Post by Eva Yojimbo » Fri Apr 15, 2011 6:23 am To me, that perspective seems really counter-intuitive. Why would Ritsuko, who hated her mother, go after Gendo if she expected to be treated with as much dismissal and disdain? Ritsuko's entire thing was that she DIDN'T want to be her mother, and, like most people have a tendency to do, she thought she was special and that her love for Gendo would overcome his coldness and obsession for Yui. For Ritsuko, there could be nothing more devastating than realizing she was no better than her mother.
